sentences.text: OK, forget.
OK?
Well, actually, I was just telling you that it was the best of time and the worst of time.
It was the best of time, because we were so close.
You know, like even now, you never forget friend like this, because we risk our life together.
And the-- and the worst of time, because it was so dangerous.
Like for example, when we meet and we-- we were young.
And we were joking, you know, like young people.
And all of sudden, one person didn't come to the-- to the meeting, where we-- and then we were worried.
And what happened is always, always, somebody was caught.
And somebody, you know, we have new people coming anyway.
But it was kind of scary, because you-- you say, well-- everybody thought, maybe next time will be me.
Oh, maybe 15, about-- a small group.
No.
No, there was-- the-- the people in charge.
To me, like, I was 8-- 17, 18.
The people in charge were 25, 26.
And to me, they were old men; [LAUGHS] and-- and some-- some older-- a few people older, too-- but mostly young people.
Well, that's what I told you.
Mostly they were Jewish.
But there was a few who were not Jewish.
Because I told you-- nobody wanted to bother-- for two reason.
First, it was very dangerous.
And then they didn't care.
Well, yeah, that's what-- I work closely with Maurice [NON-ENGLISH], which was the one who finally die under torture.
And he always liked me, because I was very shy and, you know, and very sheltered.
So I-- I was worried.
He say, you-- I know you can do it.
I know you can [LAUGHS] do it-- when he said, go and do this and this-- so-- because he has so much confidence.
I-- I-- and then after, I learned the work.
So there was another problem, which is important.
If you were a woman and you have good paper, you are safe.
But a man-- a-- a-- Jewish men were circumcised.
And then for a German, forget it.
And then in the street, they will stop man and check all the time.
And there was nothing you can do.
There is some doctor children-- no-- not daughter-- son of a doctor who has the-- their son circumcised.
And they were deported just because of this.
One-- one of us, one of these men, Pierre Muchnic, decided to have an operation, that it will look like not a circumcision, but an operation.
So if whoever did it, they was not too crazy.
But he decide to be first.
So he did it.
Nobody else did it.
They didn't want to do it.
So that it would look as though he-- he hadn't been circumcised?
Yeah, an operation, because instead of-- so that was more difficult for a man.
That's what I tell you.
They-- they deport them.
They won't take-- first, the French-- you know, only the Jew were circumcised in-- in Europe.
So what else?
Well, OK.
All these family, I was the only one in charge.
I will go.
That was my work.
Every day, I go to see some family.
And then I see what they need.
You know, I make them false paper.
Then paper for food-- you have to have a alimentation card.
And you have to have baptism certificate, marriage certificate.
Everything, we give them, but especially for people who-- who work.
The Swiss decided they will accept children but not grown-up.
But if you have a child which was under, I think, five or younger, the parents can go with it.
So every time we had-- we saw some children that we could send to Switzerland, then we will try to-- that the worst part is to talk to the parents.
You know, ask a mother to give-- to give your child is-- is a terrible thing.
So one day-- one day, I-- I have a family, I remember-- a big, big family.
And I came one day, and talked to them, and give them everything.
And then I have to bring them back false papers, something like this.
It was mother, father, a lot of children, grandparents.
They were all there.
So I came a few days later with everything they needed.
And they were not there anymore-- all gone.
Well, I don't know what the word could be.
I'll go and--
I think R-A-I-D-- raid, right?
Yeah, that's the right word.
And the-- the whole family was caught and taken away.
But there's a-- there's a little boy, about six years old, who was not there.
So he was not caught.
I say, where-- where is he?
Nobody knew.
So I said to myself, I keep thinking of that-- that child.
It bother me.
I say, I got to find it.
I put in my head to find that child.
I say, I got to find him.
So I used to go back to that street.
And-- and you have to be careful, because if you are suspicious, they-- they will, you know, they will send for the German.
But finally, after coming back a few times, somebody told me, I think-- I know you-- some people who could tell you what happened to that child.
So they told me go to such and such a place and talk to the people.
So I remember I go there, but I was terrified.
Because that could have been somebody who, you know, wanted to get me.
Because I say I could, you know, I want to find that child.
So I go there and-- and knock at the door.
And I say, well, I hope, you know.
And then I open the door, and these people pretended they didn't know anything.
I say, what child?
I say, well, you know, I'm looking for that child who was not deported.
What are you talking about?
I don't know any child.
What happened?
They were-- I was scare of them, and they were scare of me.
So they were-- they just pretended they knew nothing.
And I-- I didn't know what to do.
I talked to them.
And finally, I convinced them that, you know, I belong to the underground, and I could send that child to Switzerland.
So they told me to come back, to talk to the mother.
And then I came back, and I remember that woman-- the most beautiful person.
She had black hair, beautiful blue eyes, very tall.
And she-- I explained to her that I could-- I could take that-- that child and send him to Switzerland.
So what we have to-- the way we do it, we have to-- we give them false paper, right?
But they have to have the right-- the right name and the-- the date of birth.
And you have to-- and sew the lining.
You have to-- and unsew the lining of the coat, and put the real name inside, and sew it.
And then we never let a mother or a family go to the station, because, you know why a mother.
She can start crying, or speaking Yiddish, or God knows.
So I said, well, OK.
I meet you.
I let you know, you know, when I have a-- not all the time.
Once in a while, we have so many children.
So this-- I supposed to have one child-- me.
So then I-- I told her when.
So I said, well, you meet me.
We-- we decided to meet me in the street-- close to the station, but not by the station.
I go there.
And I waited and waited.
She didn't come.
So I said to myself, I guess she didn't trust me.
And I was already very sad.
And then all of a sudden, she appear with a, you know, a six-years-old boy in her hand and a tiny little-- maybe three, two or three in the other hand.
And she walk to me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [LAUGHS]
I always get emotional.
And she say, please.
Take my child, too.
And then she gave me her child.
You know the courage-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and the courage it take for a mother to give a child up?
Now, I didn't understand this that much.
And after I had myself two daughter, I don't think I will have the courage to give it.
I'm not sure I could-- I could give my child.
Because after, that's all.
They don't know anything anymore.
No.
Because it was out of my hand after.

I bring the child to the station, and then somebody else, another young woman like me, young lady, will dress as a nun or as a nurse, and-- and will bring these to-- close to Switzerland, which was very dangerous.
Because half of these children not even speak French if they are-- if the German are talk to them.
So most of the time, it was successful.
But sometimes it was not.
No.
I don't know what happened to the mother.
And then I have another story like this.
I was in charge of a couple who-- they were in hiding, and they-- they give birth-- and I think to a six month child-- never went out.
They never went out.
And then when I-- I talked to them, and I told them, look.
I can send it to Switzerland-- you-- you know, your husband and your child.
Oh, they were so happy, they said.
I said, well, I tell you when to get ready.
So I came and told her, you-- you know, now you have to get ready.
And the woman fainted.
She didn't want.
She didn't have the courage.
And they were deported.
They live where there were some Russian-- white Russian.
And they were deported.
They never came back.
This is a story of [LAUGHS] Switzerland.
Well, they all get-- you know, we know from, you know.
One person tell the other.
One person in hiding tell-- tell the other.
I have a man in night who stay in a cellar for two years.
And a cellar in France is not like here.
You have mice.
You have-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
What you cook with, what you put fire with-- I forget the name.
Anyway, he was living there all this time.
And the people used to-- you know, it was terrible that there were a lot of doctor and lawyer, and they were in hiding for so long that they became nobody.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think I will have survived in hiding.
It must be terrible to just stay in one room.
And-- and then I was-- when I-- you know, they asked me what happened and all kind of-- some people say they were so happy to see me.
And they used to say, here come the angel.
Oh, yeah.
Everybody-- everybody I took care of have to have false paper.
Well, they use them if-- no.
They didn't use them.
Well, sometimes they went out to buy food and things like this.
But it was a very, very dangerous time.
Well, that's another thing.
That's another thing.
There was-- well, very-- especially in Nice.
Now most of the collaborator were Russian-- Russian-- white Russian.
And the German didn't recognize between Mediterranean face, like Italian face or-- or Jewish face.
You know, they were dark hair and everything.
But the Russian, they knew.
They could smell.
They could smell them.
And then, you know, it's not that difficult, because you can see in their face.
When you are scared, you walk.
So there was two-- that's another story.
There was two terrible men there-- Russian.
They have been given-- they have been arrested 1,000-- oh, no-- hundred and hundred of family.
I mean, they were-- they were killer.
I mean, if you give a family-- first, if you give a Jew, you get enough money to live for a month.
So they were making a lot of money.
So when-- we-- we decided to eliminate them, you know.
So there were three young men.
They were the killer.
Like me, I never wear a gun, you know, to have-- but we decided to-- to do something.
And I was all for it.
So you used to knew who they were and-- and look for the way they live, where they live, what they do.
And then the-- the two worst one-- one was kill and the other-- when-- when they-- I-- oh, I forget to tell you.
When I was in-- in-- in school, there was-- I was-- I had a friend who live with a Russian, a white Russian.
And he knew me, you know.
And one day, when I change my name, he saw me.
He says, oh, Michelin.
Where-- where do you live now?
So I was very uncomfortable-- with friend.
He say, why don't you come and live with us?
It's very, very safe.
You won't have to worried.
[LAUGHS]
So one day, I saw my friend, this-- this woman from-- from art school.
And she's in bicycle.
She's all in black.
So I say, what's the matter with you?
She said, they kill him-- I mean, him-- George.
I say, why they kill him?
Well, they say he did something wrong, but it's not true, and everything.
So I was bothered.
When I met everybody in the evening, I say, well, who did you kill?
Who was it?
He said, the worst one.
And it-- this was that man who asked me to go live-- to go and live with them, Serge.
Serge, his name was.
So I was lucky.
Well, people like me-- young people, you know.
They were mostly my age, maybe a little older.
Well, most-- the worst one.
And then after from-- 1,000, maybe, I don't know-- a month.
It came down to 100.
They got scared now.
You know, the-- the-- when they saw this, you know, what happened to them.
They-- now they were not so happy to denounce people.
Oh, yes, of course.
They were my friend, yeah-- Ernest-- Ernest, Zizi, and Mark.
[LAUGHS]
Well, Ernest [NON-ENGLISH], Mark Levi, and Ziggy Porilez.
Yeah, I remember them.
Because I forget some of the name.
Yeah.
That was dangerous.
You have to be very careful.
Well, I told you, we meet in-- first, for dinner, we meet.
But sometime we stop it.
And everybody was living on their own.
I was living first with a-- a-- a friend of mine who live in Washington now.
And we lived together.
But it became too dangerous, so we separated.
You know, if one was caught-- like for example, if one day we live, most of us there.
And then Ernest was caught.
And then we had to move, because I-- we know that they will get tortured, and they may talk.
So we moved.
OK, my mother and father-- well, my father was born in Russia.
So he has an accent.
So it was not easy for-- to make him false paper, I had to go to the-- I don't know.
It's called-- it's some kind of library-- and find a-- a white Russian, who-- who is about my father, you know, height.
And my father looked like the pope-- blue-- blue eyes, and very blond, and the same age.
Sometime it takes days to find this kind of paper.
And finally, my parents left to-- to Carros outside of Nice.
And my brother finally, he went to the Maquis for a while.
And things happened, and-- and he had to come back.
And I send him to Switzerland, because the problem, he was 16-- 15, I think.
He was 15.
And he was-- he was too old.
So he was to wear, you know, short pants.
And he finally went through.
And my sister was in the United State.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Did you-- were you aware of what was going on in-- in other places?
Did you have--
Oh, yeah.
Well, the news-- yeah, of course.
And then we listen to the, you know, Radio London and everything.
Yeah.
I live alone most of the time.
But there is much more to say.
But I want to-- I want to tell you, like, my state of mind, right?
Well, I was still young, you know.
And I wanted to look good anyway.
And I remember I had-- I order some shoes, these platform-- covered.
I wanted it covered with certain color.
And then they told you-- they told me it will take two weeks.
And I said to myself, oh, I hope I will wear them before I get caught.
I was-- I wanted to wear these shoes before I get caught.
You see what my state of mind?
That's when-- and then another time, once in a while, you know, to forget, I went to see a movie.
And in the movie, you feel when-- when it's the end of the movie.
You can feel it.
And when I felt the end of the movie, I started to be oppress-- you know, like, I say, oh my God.
I still have to go out and live, you know.
How long will I-- how long will I, you know, will I survive?
So that was the feeling I have.
I was-- I didn't want the end of the movie.
I didn't want to go back to-- to this life.
And then when I came out of the movie, somebody stole the wheel of my bicycle, [LAUGHS] to make me feel badder.
Because it was very difficult to find bicycle.
But of course, the underground give me another bicycle.
And there is much more, but-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that I remember, yeah, you know.
Well, what I want to say is the-- the priest, and the minister, and some people in the government were very helpful.
They were doing false paper-- [NON-ENGLISH].
There is the man in charge of the Protestant, you know, the minister-- who is quite known.
I think he is known in this country, too-- [NON-ENGLISH].
Well, I was his-- I was the one who come to see him.
And he has his people for-- for-- like he has Jew, and he has other people for other thing.
And I will come.
And he will give me, without explanation, he is giving me the picture.
And he say, I need so many false paper.
Then I will-- and I didn't ask him anything.
And he didn't ask me anything.
And I will come back and-- and give him the false paper.
And one thing-- they told you never fill out the paper.
But I go to these people.
Half of them were not even French.
They didn't know to write well.
So then I fill the paper anyway.
So like I tell you, I always come a few minutes before or after the Gestapo.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And one day, one day before-- before really, I was very involved.
I was on the beach in Nice, and the German follow me, you know, and keep bothering me.
He wanted to talk to me, everything.
I couldn't get rid of him.
So I got annoyed and I say, why don't you leave me alone?
I'm Jewish.
So he look at me and he say, don't worry, mademoiselle.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Don't worry.
The wheel turn.
That was interesting.
Wasn't it a risk for you to say that you were Jewish?
Yeah, I took a risk.
[LAUGHS] It was a risk.
I got fed up.
I didn't know to-- I took a chance.
So he left me alone.
But he told me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and my parents were working for-- making uniform for the underground-- what you call the Maquis.
See, our resistance was more helping people.
It was as dangerous as being in the Maquis, with arm, and, you know, sabotage, the railroad, and putting bomb, and everything.
So my parents were making uniform for them in the Maquis.
No.
We did-- we did only saving people.
Well, later on, some of the young people, like the killer.
You know.
They moved to-- well, they moved to-- a few of them moved to Paris.
And a lot of them were-- were caught.
A lot of them were caught just few more-- when the-- the-- the American, you know, were already close to Paris.
And they were caught.
And they were all-- well, it's-- it's a whole story.
They were all-- went to Drancy, which is the place.

And they-- at the last minute, the man in charge of that concentration camp nearest Paris, it was so important for him to-- to get rid of the Jew that-- already the German were-- the only thing they wanted was to-- to leave to go back to Germany.
So all the train were for the German to go back to Germany.
But this [NON-ENGLISH], I think, it was, this man wanted any price to deport the last-- especially my friend who were in the underground.
So he finally give two pig and a few chicken to get a-- a train, just for the Jew.
And they put that train in the back.

And that's a whole story, because somebody wrote a book, a book called [FRENCH], And Jules Dassin, which is the man who-- who did the-- the plane-- you know, this-- the French plane which is so-- Mirage?
Jules Dassin.
He's the one who did the-- well, if l look at my book.
He did-- I think the Mirage.
He did the-- this-- know the plane, the quick plane?
The Concorde?
Yeah.
Well, this guy, he's the one who developed this.
He was in that-- he was a very known engineer.
He was in that train.
And then a lot of people, they finally put a dummy.
The-- the Germans say you cannot escape.
There is somebody at-- on top of the train, and you will be shot.
They put a dummy.
And they-- somebody give them, just before to leave, give them a-- something to cut the-- you know, the window.
And they throw that dummy, and nothing happened.
So whoever wanted to escape, decided to do it.
But he didn't want to.
Well, and there was a lot of them escaped, actually.
All my friend escaped.
I mean, they throw themselves when the-- when the train were not that quick.
They throw themself through the-- and they-- they escape.
Yeah.
The German-- the American were already close to Paris.
OK.
In the meantime, I could find out the name of that--
